# Private Inference for Agents — Live Status

**Hermes wraps every outgoing chat request in an httpx transport that encrypts the conversation to a public key advertised by the provider's attested gateway.** A ✅ on this page means that gateway's attestation bundle verifies end-to-end — it does **not** mean the model backend that actually runs inference is verified. See [amiller/devproof-audits-guide](https://github.com/amiller/devproof-audits-guide) for the full gap analysis, especially the [NEAR Private Chat attestation-gap analysis](https://github.com/amiller/devproof-audits-guide/blob/main/case-studies/near-private-chat/ATTESTATION-GAP-ANALYSIS.md) which tracks the primary limitation below.

> **This page is a workflow artifact, not a hand-written doc.**
> Regenerated by [`tee-attestation-report.yml`](../.github/workflows/tee-attestation-report.yml) on a [scheduled cron](../.github/workflows/tee-attestation-report.yml#L5) (13:17 UTC daily) and on every push that touches the transport or verifier. Each run re-executes [`scripts/attestation_report.py`](../scripts/attestation_report.py) against live providers and commits the result back to the branch — the table below is whatever the most recent run observed.

[![TEE Attestation](https://github.com/amiller/hermes-agent/actions/workflows/tee-attestation-report.yml/badge.svg?branch=feat/near-ai-attestation)](https://github.com/amiller/hermes-agent/actions/workflows/tee-attestation-report.yml)

## Upstream integration

- **[NousResearch/hermes-agent#12201](https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/12201)** — Add NEAR AI + RedPill TDX attestation and the in-process E2EE httpx transport. Branch `feat/near-ai-attestation`.
- Transport lives at [`hermes_cli/e2ee_transport.py`](../hermes_cli/e2ee_transport.py) · verifier at [`hermes_cli/attestation.py`](../hermes_cli/attestation.py) · script at [`scripts/attestation_report.py`](../scripts/attestation_report.py).

## What this proves

Modern agents leak their full context — memory, credentials, intermediate reasoning, tool outputs — to whichever model they call. This workflow verifies, on every run, that the models Hermes talks to are running inside Intel TDX + NVIDIA confidential-compute enclaves and that the CLI's encryption key is bound to those specific enclaves.

If a row below is ❌, the CLI refuses to send plaintext to that model and the `/model` picker hides it. No fallbacks.

## Known limitations that undermine the guarantee

What the ✅s actually verify: the provider's **gateway** (cloud-api for NEAR AI, RedPill's dstack gateway for Phala) is running attested code in a TEE, and its `signing_public_key` is bound to that TEE's TDX quote. The CLI then encrypts conversations to that key. What the ✅s do **not** verify:

- **The inner boundary — backends aren't attested by the gateway.** The gateway fetches `/v1/attestation/report` from each model backend but parses the JSON and throws the result away without verifying the TDX quote, checking `compose_hash`, or validating the backend `signing_public_key`. This is the actual architectural gap. NEAR's own [Issue #224](https://github.com/nearai/cloud-api/issues/224) has been open since 2025-12-03 acknowledging that cloud-api should verify backends; 4+ months later it remains open. Full tracing: [NEAR Private Chat attestation-gap analysis](https://github.com/amiller/devproof-audits-guide/blob/main/case-studies/near-private-chat/ATTESTATION-GAP-ANALYSIS.md). The signing key we encrypt to is effectively trust-on-first-use from unverified JSON inside the gateway.
- **Operator controls routing.** The gateway selects backends by looking up `inference_url` in its database — a value the operator can change without breaking attestation. An operator (or anyone with DB access) can point the URL at a logging relay that forwards to a real model; the gateway accepts it as long as the relay returns any valid-looking JSON on the attestation endpoint.
- **Verifier caveats we inherit.** Our verifier is built on Phala's `private-ai-verifier`, which still decodes NVIDIA GPU JWTs and Intel Trust Authority JWTs with `verify_signature=False` (acknowledged TODOs), and trusts a local `dstack-verifier` sidecar without authentication. See [LEARNINGS.md](https://github.com/amiller/devproof-audits-guide/blob/main/LEARNINGS.md) for the broader pattern catalogue.
- **Hermes-the-app is Stage 0.** If you're running the Hermes CVM itself (not just the CLI), the [hermes case study](https://github.com/amiller/devproof-audits-guide/blob/main/case-studies/hermes/DEVPROOF-REPORT.md) documents Pha KMS opacity, mutable image tags, and no upgrade log — these are separate from the inference-attestation gap above, but compound with it.

In short: the cryptographic layer on this page is working exactly as designed; it's the design that currently stops at the gateway. The per-model ✅s are necessary but not sufficient.

## What's verified per row

1. Fetch the provider's live attestation bundle (`GET /v1/attestation/report?model=…&nonce=…`).
2. TDX quote cryptographically verified via Phala's verifier API.
3. `report_data` binds the CVM's signing address and our fresh nonce.
4. NVIDIA GPU attested via NRAS (`/v3/attest/gpu`).
5. `signing_public_key` derives to `signing_address` (keccak of pubkey last 20 bytes).

A ✅ means all five held **at this run's timestamp**. The CLI wraps outgoing chat content in an httpx transport that encrypts to that `signing_public_key`, so only the attested model sees the plaintext.
